exemplar.title: âTo what extent do Johnson and Johnson corporate social responsibility (CSR) policies affect their brand equity in the United States between 2022 and 2023?â

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, time-bounded and well connected to the analysis
Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with comprehensive explanation of context and relevance
Methodology draws on an appropriate range of secondary sources and analytical tools with evidence of informed selection
weaknesses: Analysis occasionally drifts into historical material outside the specified 2022â23 window, diluting focus
Methodology section lacks explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria and deeper justification of source selection
Linkages between specific CSR metrics and brand-equity measures in the defined period could be more explicitly maintained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation demonstrates accurate and effective communication of the topic, a well-focused and clearly stated research question, and an appropriate range of relevant methods. Minor digressions into events outside the time frame and limited methodological justification prevent a perfect score, placing the work firmly in the upper band (5/6).

strengths: Terminology (CSR, brand equity, ROI, inventory turnover) is used accurately and consistently
Source material from annual reports and academic articles is clearly relevant and integrated into the argument
Subject knowledge is coherent and supported by appropriate use of models (Carrollâs Pyramid, SWOT)
weaknesses: Reliance on popular media and non-scholarly sources in places undermines the academic rigor of some assertions
Occasional mis-scaling in ratio calculations and missing citations for benchmarks reduce precision
Conceptual explanations of brand-equity measurement remain surface-level and would benefit from deeper theoretical grounding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay applies source material appropriately, demonstrates clear and coherent subject knowledge, and uses relevant terminology consistently. Occasional reliance on less rigorous sources and minor technical inaccuracies limit the performance to a solid mid-band score (4/6).

strengths: Research draws on a wide range of quantitative and qualitative data directly relevant to the research question
Analytical tools (CSR pyramid, SWOT, financial ratios) are applied effectively to build a coherent argument
The line of reasoning is logical and culminates in a clear conclusion that CSR positively impacts brand equity
weaknesses: Critical evaluation remains largely general, with limited interrogation of underlying assumptions
Causal claims between CSR investments and brand metrics are asserted without rigorous empirical validation
Discussion and evaluation do not fully explore alternative perspectives or industry benchmarks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows consistently strong research quality and clear analytical thinking, using multiple strands of evidence to support conclusions. Although the evaluation could be more critically nuanced and empirical support for causality strengthened, the overall critical-thinking performance merits the top of the 4-6 band (6/12).

strengths: The report follows the prescribed structure with all required elements (title page, RQ, word count, bibliography) in place
Headings, subheadings, and logical organization guide the reader effectively through each section
Tables and figures are generally well integrated to support the narrative
weaknesses: Residual HTML tags and inconsistent indentation appear in several tables and lists
Some bibliographic entries and table captions lack uniform formatting and completeness
Minor pagination inconsistencies and occasional layout oversights interrupt the flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All presentation requirements are met and the structure is clear and logical. Minor formatting oversightsâvisible code artifacts and inconsistent reference stylingâprevent a flawless finish, resulting in a top-band score (4/4).

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Clarity of the RQ** â The research question is explicitly stated, time-bounded, and focused on a single firm and market, demonstrating an adequate level of precision.  
**Connection to discussion** â Most sections (Carrollâs Pyramid, SWOT, ratios) aim to address the stated RQ, showing a clearâthough not always sustainedâlink between question and analysis.  
**Shortfalls in sustained focus** â Extended coverage of historic cases (1982 Tylenol, 2019-24 talc lawsuits) moves beyond the 2022-23 window and dilutes focus. These digressions indicate only partial, not full, focus; hence the upper mark (4) of the 3-4 band is appropriate rather than the highest band.

**Argument development** â The student presents a coherent line of reasoning culminating in a conclusion that CSR positively affects J&Jâs brand equity, integrating multiple strands of evidence.  
**Evaluation elements** â Some recognition of limitations (e.g., absence of primary data, potential greenwashing, short-term cost implications) shows an attempt at critical evaluation.  
**Depth of critique** â Evaluation tends to be general and often reiterates findings rather than critically weighing alternative viewpoints or assessing the robustness of evidence. This situates the work within the 4-6 band for discussion/evaluation, meriting a score 4.

**Clarity and coherence** â The student demonstrates a sound grasp of CSR, brand equity, and financial ratios, explaining concepts such as Carrollâs Pyramid, SWOT, stock turnover and ROI with reasonable accuracy and integrating them into the discussion.  
**Use of sources to support knowledge** â Data from J&J reports and secondary literature underpin the narrative, indicating that knowledge is derived from, and generally supported by, external material.  
**Limitations** â Some conceptual explanations remain surface-level (e.g., limited exploration of the complexities of brand equity measurement) and occasional factual imprecision appears. Nonetheless, the knowledge base is organised and largely correct, justifying a high mark in the 3-4 band (score 4).

**Terminology usage** â Key business termsâCSR, brand equity, stock turnover ratio, ROI, greenwashing, stakeholder, etc.âare woven consistently into the essay, generally spelled and applied correctly.  
**Conceptual accuracy** â Models such as Carrollâs Pyramid and SWOT are described accurately and applied to J&Jâs context, showing an understanding of their meaning and scope.  
**Minor issues** â There are instances of awkward phrasing and occasional mis-scaling of ratios (e.g., interpreting ROI without time value), but these do not seriously impede comprehension. The predominance of correct terminology positions the work at the upper end of the 3-4 band; score 4 is awarded.

**Demonstrated analysis** â The essay applies recognised analytical tools (Carrollâs Pyramid, SWOT, financial ratios) to consider how CSR initiatives may influence brand equity, providing calculations and qualitative commentary.  
**Support for conclusions** â Links between CSR activities and changes in financial/brand metrics are suggested but evidence is often inferential rather than rigorously demonstrated; causality is asserted more than proved.  
**Depth and sophistication** â While analysis moves beyond description, it seldom interrogates assumptions or considers alternative interpretations. Consequently, the work sits solidly in the 4-6 band for analysis depth but at the lower end; a score 4 reflects clear yet not fully developed analytical thinking.

**Relevance and appropriateness** â Most cited materials (annual reports, earnings presentations, reputable business sources) align with the analysis of CSR and brand equity, and specific figures (e.g., ROI, inventory levels) are used in calculations, indicating largely relevant application.  
**Integration into argument** â Source data are incorporated to populate Carrollâs Pyramid, SWOT tables and ratio calculations, showing that the student moves beyond mere quotation.  
**Areas for improvement** â Some assertions about consumer perceptions and causal links between CSR and brand equity lack direct supporting evidence; several sources are descriptive web articles rather than scholarly studies. Overall application is âmostly relevant and appropriate,â meriting a mark of 4 within the 3-4 band.

**Range and relevance of sources/methods** â The essay draws on a reasonable spread of secondary data: company reports, financial statements, news outlets and academic articles. Business-management tools (Carrollâs Pyramid, SWOT, ROI, inventory ratio) are appropriate to the topic.  
**Evidence of informed selection** â The student justifies the choice of each tool and recognises some limitations (e.g., subjectivity of SWOT, historic nature of financial ratios). This shows awareness of methodological implications.  
**Gaps and limitations** â No primary research is attempted; the treatment of data sources is descriptive and occasionally relies on popular media of varying academic quality. Documentation of how data were selected or verified is limited. These factors fit the 3-4 band, with a score of 4 indicating the stronger end of âgenerally relevant â¦ some evidence of informed selection.â

**Breadth and relevance** â The student gathers data from company financial statements, reputable business publications and CSR reports, providing adequate quantitative and qualitative information linked to the RQ.  
**Alignment to RQ** â The sources clearly relate to J&Jâs CSR activities and brand performance in 2022-23, demonstrating relevance.  
**Depth limitations** â Absence of primary research and reliance on public reports limit insight, and some sources (blogs, news snippets) vary in academic rigour. Overall, the research is stronger than merely âadequateâ but lacks the consistency and critical selection expected for the top bands, so a mid-high score of 5 within the 4-6 band is appropriate.

**Presentation standards met** â The essay includes a title page, word count, page numbers and a formatted bibliography. Tables, headings and basic spacing enhance readability.  
**Inconsistencies** â Residual markdown/HTML tags in tables and occasional misaligned columns indicate that layout conventions are not flawlessly executed. Citation style shows uneven formatting, and some tables spill across pages awkwardly.  
**Overall judgement** â Most layout and formatting requirements are satisfied, but execution lacks polish and full accuracy; thus the work sits at the lower end of the 3-4 band, warranting a score 3.

**Clarity and completeness** â The student presents the topic of Johnson & Johnsonâs CSR impact on brand equity in the United States (2022-2023) early in the essay, providing sufficient context about the firm, key controversies and the relevance of CSR to brand equity.  
**Comprehensiveness of explanation** â The introduction explains why the issue is worth investigating, links it to course concepts (e.g., CSR, brand equity) and outlines the analytical tools to be used.  
**Minor limitations** â Although the explanation occasionally digresses into historical events outside the time frame, the overall communication remains accurate and effective. These small lapses prevent a flawless performance but still place the work firmly in the 5-6 band; a mark of 5 best reflects these strengths with minor shortcomings.

**Logical organisation** â The essay follows the prescribed EE structure: title page, table of contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion and bibliography. Headings and sub-headings guide the reader logically through the argument.  
**Adherence to EE conventions** â Required elements (title, RQ, word count, bibliography) are present, and sectioning facilitates understanding.  
**Minor lapses** â Some pagination or page references appear inconsistent, and HTML tags remain visible in tables, indicating slight formatting oversights; however, the overall structure is clearly appropriate, justifying the top band (3-4) with a score 4.

annotations.comment: The inventory turnover benchmark range (5â10) is stated without citation. Reference the methodological source to validate its applicability to the healthcare sector.

The research question is clearly stated, time-bounded and specific, providing a strong anchor for the investigation. Ensure all subsequent analysis remains tightly linked to this question to maintain focus.
Ensure consistent list formatting so that each bibliographic entry appears cohesively and avoids mid-entry line breaks.
The word count of 3819 is within the 4000-word limit, demonstrating compliance with formatting requirements.
Sub-section entries show inconsistent indentation and font weight. Standardize indentation and heading styles to improve navigability.
Analysis asserts that economic responsibilities boost profitability; include specific financial metrics (e.g., ROI improvements) to substantiate these claims.
The 2022 calculation is presented clearly, but ensure consistency in significant figures; explain rounding decisions to maintain precision.
The 2023 turnover ratio is correctly computed; discuss possible error margins or confidence intervals to reflect quarterly data variability.
In the SWOT table, the threats section cites regulatory pressure but omits specific U.S. regulatory changes from 2022â2023. Incorporate precise examples for relevance.
The caption for Table 1 is too generic. Enhance it by including the date range and data sources to make it self-sufficient.
The title succinctly frames the focus on J&Jâs CSR and brand equity; consider adding a subtitle to specify the geographic and temporal boundaries for greater precision.

The historical context from 1982 and 2024 talc lawsuits provides background but partly falls outside the 2022â2023 focus, which dilutes the investigationâs scope. Consider trimming or explicitly linking these events to your period.
The methodology section outlines secondary research but lacks justification for source selection criteria and evaluation of potential biases. Define clear inclusion/exclusion criteria.
The student relies on popular media sources for historical events; integrating peer-reviewed or academic studies would bolster credibility and support assertions more robustly.
The CSR definition is well introduced, but its connection to specific brand equity metrics in 2022â2023 could be made more explicit to strengthen the methodological link.
Residual HTML tags are visible in the table of contents, distracting the reader. Clean up HTML artifacts and ensure consistent styling for a polished presentation.
Restating the research question reinforces focus; reposition this summary to conclude the introduction for stronger narrative flow and reader guidance.
The background section offers rich context but is text-heavy. Introduce subheadings or bullet points within this section to improve readability and structure.
Discussion of stakeholder partnerships and brand advocacy is insightful but remains descriptive. Include citation-based evidence or metrics to substantiate claims about their effect on brand equity.
Noting the subjectivity of SWOT is helpful, but the discussion of human factors is underdeveloped. Reflect on how excluding these aspects may bias your analysis.
Quantitative data on market share and profit is valuable; however, specific sources for these figures (e.g., original annual reports) should be cited to enhance verifiability.
The methods reference course units but do not explain how these theoretical frameworks were operationalized. Clarify the steps taken to apply Unit 1.3 and Unit 3 tools to your data.
This paragraph ends abruptly and leaves the limitation discussion incomplete. Ensure all points are fully articulated and linked back to your research question.

The formula is correct but should use LaTeX notation for consistency, e.g., $$
	ext{Inventory Turnover} = 
\frac{	ext{COGS}}{	ext{Average Inventory}}
$$.
Attributing causality between CSR investments and turnover changes requires empirical evidence. Consider statistical analysis to distinguish causation from correlation.
The conclusion reiterates findings well but could be strengthened by explicitly linking conclusions back to the research question and outlining areas for future research.
Consider adding a dedicated reflection section detailing decision-making and adjustments made during your research to demonstrate deeper engagement.
Bibliography entries display inconsistent formatting (italics, URL styles). Adopt a single citation style (e.g., APA) and apply it uniformly.
The timeline of talc events extends into 2024, beyond the 2022â2023 focus. Refocus on events within your defined period or justify the relevance of earlier/ later cases.
This multi-page table disrupts continuity. Consider splitting it into smallertables with clear captions or reformatting to maintain reader engagement.
You present litigation costs effectively, but the direct impact on brand equity metrics is missing. Connect these financial liabilities to changes in consumer perception or brand strength.
Table 1 lists numerous CSR initiatives but suffers from inconsistent detail levels. Standardize description lengths and specificity for balanced comparative analysis.
The explanation of Carrollâs Pyramid is clear, but citing Carrollâs original work or a foundational CSR text would strengthen the theoretical underpinning.
The CSR pyramid table merges rows with complex spans; clarify row/column boundaries to prevent misinterpretation of responsibilities and analyses.
An orphaned row for workforce development appears detached from the main pyramid table. Remove such elements or integrate them correctly to maintain coherence.
The synthesis paragraph effectively distills pyramid findings; to deepen critical insight, compare J&Jâs CSR performance with industry benchmarks.
The SWOT introduction notes limitations but lacks direct linkage to 2022â2023 data. Explicitly tie each SWOT factor to your periodâs evidence to maintain focus.
Several online sources lack complete access dates or publication details. Ensure each reference includes author, date, and access date for academic rigor.
Some bibliographic entries are split across lines, breaking continuity. Consolidate each reference into a single, logically wrapped entry.
SWOT synthesis offers strategy implications but lacks critical prioritization of threats and concrete mitigation proposals. Deepen evaluation by ranking risks.

The ROI formula is correctly stated; use LaTeX for clarity: $$
	ext{ROI} = \frac{	ext{Net Income}}{	ext{Total Revenue}} \times 100\%
$$.
Reporting a 24% ROI is insightful; compare it to industry peersâ ROI in 2022 to contextualize J&Jâs performance.
The decline to 17.7% ROI merits analysis of underlying factors (e.g., increased CSR costs, market shifts). Suggest further investigation into these drivers.
The final summary is comprehensive yet descriptive; integrate a critical self-evaluation of methodological limitations and potential biases.
